coredata.dc:title: Hydrogen bonding effect on pH-sensing mechanism of carbon dots
coredata.dc:creator: Minh Hoang, Nguyen
Thi Bich Ngoc, Nguyen
Thi Lan Huong, Phan
Dao, Quang-Duy
Nam Anh, Tran
Thi Hai Linh, Dang
Nguyen, VanThan
Tuan Tu, Le
Xuan Nang, Ho
Dao, Van-Duong
coredata.dc:description: Recently, carbon dots (CDs) have received huge attention from scientists around the globe due to their unique properties, including excellent optical properties, good photobleaching, good biocompatibility, and ease of production. Notably, CDs exhibit novel results in pH sensing applications in water and intracellular environments due to their excellent anti-interfering ability and photostability. Herein, we successfully synthesized CDs by hydrothermal method using glucose and citric acid as precursors. The formation and breaking of hydrogen bonding play an important role in pH-sensing mechanisms of CDs. By using Raman signal of the hydrogen bonding in the photoluminescence (PL) spectra of CDs, we investigate here the effect of hydrogen bonding on the pH-sensing of CDs. It is expected that the mechanism of pH-sensing on the PL signal could be unraveled. As the results, the hydrogen bonding significantly reduced the PL intensity of CDs in a pH range of 7–11 but it did not influence the PL of CDs in higher pH conditions. Based on the interaction between hydrogen bonding and CDs, a new model for the pH-sensing mechanism of CDs was proposed. This work sheds new light on the mechanism of pH-sensing on the PL signal and suggests a new application of CDs in moisture and water sensors for air, soil, food, and commercial chemicals.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
pH sensing
Hydrogen bonding
Quenching effect
Red-shift

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) HR-TEM image of CDs, the particle size distribution of CDs (the inset). (b) A digital image of CDs under 365 nm excitation. (c) FT-IR spectrum of CDs.

(a) PL spectra of CDs at a concentration of 0.01 mg/ml in pH of 7. (b) PL intensity and position of maximum emission peak of CDs in different pH environments. (c) Raman signals in different pH environments. PL spectra of CDs in pH of 12 (d), 13 (e), and 14 (f).
UV–Vis spectra of CDs (0.01 mg/ml) in different pH environments.
The Raman spectrum of CD solutions under pH 7 – 14.
Schematic of hydrogen bonding network surrounding CD particles and the energy diagrams of CDs in response to pH change.

head.ce:title: Hydrogen bonding effect on pH-sensing mechanism of carbon dots

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Recently, carbon dots (CDs) have received huge attention from scientists around the globe due to their unique properties, including excellent optical properties, good photobleaching, good biocompatibility, and ease of production. Notably, CDs exhibit novel results in pH sensing applications in water and intracellular environments due to their excellent anti-interfering ability and photostability. Herein, we successfully synthesized CDs by hydrothermal method using glucose and citric acid as precursors. The formation and breaking of hydrogen bonding play an important role in pH-sensing mechanisms of CDs. By using Raman signal of the hydrogen bonding in the photoluminescence (PL) spectra of CDs, we investigate here the effect of hydrogen bonding on the pH-sensing of CDs. It is expected that the mechanism of pH-sensing on the PL signal could be unraveled. As the results, the hydrogen bonding significantly reduced the PL intensity of CDs in a pH range of 7–11 but it did not influence the PL of CDs in higher pH conditions. Based on the interaction between hydrogen bonding and CDs, a new model for the pH-sensing mechanism of CDs was proposed. This work sheds new light on the mechanism of pH-sensing on the PL signal and suggests a new application of CDs in moisture and water sensors for air, soil, food, and commercial chemicals.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs), a new member of the carbon-based materials family, are categorized as 0D materials since their three dimensions are usually less than 10 nm [1]. There are several advantages of CDs over traditional quantum dots, including excellent photostability, high quantum yields, low toxicity, good biocompatibility, and ease of production via multiple methods [2–5]. Therefore, CDs are being used in a variety of applications, including ion detection [6], bio-imaging [7–9], fingerprint [10], pH sensing [6–8,11], wastewater treatment [12], solar to steam generation [13], solar concentrator [14–17] and solar cells [18].

The use of CDs as a pH sensor has received a lot of attention in recent years because of its several advantages, including fast response time, sensitivity to pH changes, high accuracy, and the ability to monitor a wide pH range [19,20]. CDs display two separate processes in response to pH changes, including increasing or decreasing emission intensity [21–23] and the red or blue shift of the emission peak [24,25], which relates to changes in energy levels [11], aggregation [26], and electron transfer [27]. These hypotheses are based on the interaction of OH– and H+ ions acting directly on the functional groups of CDs, changing the surface states. CDs have various functional groups on the surface, such as -C=O, –OH, and -C-OH, which promote the formation of hydrogen bonds between CD particles and theirsurrounding water molecules. In addition, the hydrogen bonding had a huge impact on the photoluminescence (PL) properties of the CDs. It is known that there is a formation of hydrogen bonding between the functional groups on CDs such as -C-OH and -C=O with H2O which rigidifies the structure of the CDs and leads to an increase in fluorescence and phosphorescence intensity [6,28]. In contrast, hydrogen bonds were also responsible for PL quenching effect [21,29].

Although there are many reports proposed to explain the pH sensing mechanisms of CDs, most of them explain only one single mechanism. In fact, no proposed theory can explain all sensing mechanisms. In this study, we proposed a hypothesis which related to the hydrogen bonding network between CDs and water molecules to explain the two aforementioned sensing mechanisms of CDs. The Raman signal of hydrogen bonding was conducted to investigate the effect of hydrogen bonding on PL spectra of CDs. The relationship between the hydrogen bonding and the PL mechanism of CDs was elucidated by observing the change in their signal under different pH conditions. This study demonstrates the huge potential of CDs for measuring the water content of commercial chemicals and for monitoring moisture levels in the air, soil, food, and other environments.

Fig. 1 a shows the HR-TEM image and the size distribution of CDs. CDs are uniform in size, with an average size of 4.02 nm and the largest proportion of 4 nm (inset picture). Under the UV light, CDs emitted bright blue light (Fig. 1b). The prepared CDs contained a large number of functional groups, as displayed in Fig. 1c. The strong peak at 3300 cm−1 and its shoulder peak at 2900 cm−1 correspond to –OH and CH2 groups, respectively [30]. The two sharp and strong peaks at 1640 and 980 cm−1 were ascribed to the carboxyl (C=O) and primary alcohol groups (C-OH), respectively [31]. The vibrations of C-O and C-O-C groups were displayed in the range from 1100 to 1500 cm−1 [31,32]. The presence of hydrophilic groups (OH and C-OH) on CD’s surface led to good water solubility and dispersibility of the CD particles. Furthermore, they also facilitated the formation of a hydrogen bonding surrounding CD particles.

Fig. 2 a shows the fluorescence emission spectra of the CD sample at a concentration of 0.01 mg/ml in a pH 7 solution. As can be seen, the prepared CDs exhibited excitation-dependent emission, with the maximum emission peak at 439 nm under 330 nm excitation. When the excitation wavelength shifted from 330 to 380 nm, the emission peak was slightly red-shifted and gradually decreased in intensity. This excitation-dependent emission property might be ascribed to the multiple energy levels created by the abundant functional groups on CD surface [33,34]. Using the comparison method (Figure S1), the quantum yield was calculated to be 25.1 %, illustrating good fluorescence properties. In addition, the CD sample also shows a good photostablity. Fig. S2a shows the changes in PL spectra in high concentrations of metal ions. The CDs show minor changes in the presence of Na+, K+, and Mg2+. Meanwhile, CDs clearly show a quenching effect in the presence of Fe3+ and an enhancement effect in the presence of Pb2+. The PL intensity was slightly reduced after being irradiated under a UV lamp for 1 month (Fig. S2b).

Notably, PL spectra of CD sample clearly show the presence of the Raman signal of OH stretching which represents the formation of hydrogen bonding [35]. The Raman signal peaks shifted uniformly with the step of 10 nm which was equal to the increment in the exciting wavelength. Interestingly, the signal did not disappear at a high concentration of CD solution (0.05 mg/ml), showing the co-existence of hydrogen bonding and fluorescent CD particles (Figure S3). Notably, the Raman signal significantly decreased in ethanol and disappeared in acetone (Figure S4). Note that ethanol and acetone are less polar than water. The results confirm that CD nanoparticles created hydrogen bonds with surrounding water molecules.

The full PL spectra of CDS in response to pH changes from 7 to 11 are shown in Fig. S5. Overall, the PL spectra of CDS in the pH range of 7–11 share many similar features in their curves, emission peaks, and peak-shifting characteristics, indicating that the PL mechanism was unchanged in this pH range. As shown in Fig. 2b, the emission peak of CDs gradually increased with an increase in pH from 7 to 11. Figure S6 illustrates the linear range from pH 7 to 11 with the limits of detection value of 3.1.

At pH 12, the maximum emission peak significantly increased threefold before decreasing at higher pH values. Whereas the Raman signal exhibited the opposite trend (Figure). The Raman intensity gradually decreased when the pH was increased from 7 to 11 (Fig. 2c). Surprisingly, when the pH was higher than 12, Raman signal disappeared completely. After Raman signal disappeared at pH 12, the PL spectra widened extensively to the red region (Fig. 2d). The maximum excitation wavelength shifted from 330 nm to 380 nm, and the maximum emission peak of CDs was shifted to 452 nm under 380 nm excitation. As the pH value continually increased, the maximum emission peak exhibited a large red-shift to 487.5 and 492.5 nm at pH values of 13 and 14, respectively (Fig. 2e, f). Notably, CDs demonstrated an excitation-dependent emission wavelength at pH 14, with the emission peak shifting from 443 nm to 492.5 nm upon increasing the excitation wavelength from 330 nm to 370 nm (Fig. 3 f).

To investigate the change in the PL spectra in response to pH variation, UV–Vis spectra were estimated. Fig. 3 depicts the UV–Vis spectra of CDs at various pH levels. As can be seen, there are two absorbancepeaks at 270 and 324 nm, originating from the π → π* transition of the aromatic sp2 domain and n → π* transition of oxygen-containing groups, respectively [36]. The π → π* transition is generally regarded as the core state, whereas the n → π* transition is associated with the surface states [37]. At pH of 7, only one absorbance peak at 270 nm was observed indicatingthe core state was dominant [38]. As the pH rises to 12, the absorbance peak at 270 nm increases significantly, which was explained by the significant increase in PL intensity as mentioned above. The second absorbance peak at 324 nmstarted emerging at a pH of 13 and dominated the first peak at a pH of 14, indicating the deprotonation of oxygen-containing groups on the surface of CDs [20,39]. In addition, it created new emissive traps on the surface of CDs and a change in emission mechanism which was in agreement with the large red shift in the emission peak as described in Fig. 2.

From the data above, a hypothesis is proposed in Scheme 1 to explain pH-sensing mechanism of CDs based on the relation between the hydrogen bonding and CDs. With a pH in the range of 7 to 12, the pH-sensing mechanism is based on the breaking of hydrogen bonds which consequently leads to enhanced PL intensity. A hydrogen bonding formed between the functional groups on the surface of CDs and the water molecule reduced the PL intensity due to the loss of vibrational energy of OH stretching.

Increasing the pH value weakens the hydrogen bonding network, reducing the energy loss for vibration and charge transfer process from excited states of CDs’ surface states to water molecules [23,39]. Consequently, more excited electrons released photons during the relaxation process. Therefore, CDs exhibited enhanced PL intensity. Furthermore, new emissive states were formed on the surface of CDs during the breaking of hydrogen bonds, which was followed by the switchof the PL origin from the core to the surface state. The change in the PL origin results in a large red shift in the emission peak. In strong alkaline conditions, the pH sensing mechanism switched to the aggregation of the CD particles, promoting the aggregation-induced quenching effect and Fröster energy transfer [1,11,40,41]. Consequently, the PL intensity was gradually decreased accompanied by a red shift in the emission peak.

To investigate the change of hydrogen bonding in response to the pH change from 7 to 14, we conducted Raman spectrum of different CD solutions (Fig. 4 ). It was found that the two peaks at 1060 and 1110 cm−1 were assigned to the skeletal CCO deformation and CO stretching modes, respectively [42]. CCO deformation did not change. Meanwhile, the CO stretching significantly decreased with an increasing pH value, indicating a decrease in the hydrogen bonding between CD nanoparticles and water molecules [43]. In addition, due to the aggregation of CD nanoparticles at high pH environments [11], the CO bond was shortened, leading to the blue shift of the CO stretching [43]. The obtained result further confirmed the hypothesis in this work.

In summary, we demonstrated a novel model for the mechanism of pH-sensing CDs fabricated using hydrothermal. In particular, the pH sensing mechanism was ascribed to the breaking of hydrogen bonds between CD particles and water molecules in the low pH ranges from 7 to 12. When the pH was increased to some extent, the hydrogen bonding network weakened, leading to a significant increase in PL intensity and the formation of new emissive traps on the CD surface. In higher pH conditions, the pH-sensing mechanism switched to the aggregation of CD particles and FRET effect. These findings suggest that CDs could be exploited to fabricate moisture and water sensors for air, soil, food, and commercial chemicals.
Glucose, citric acid, ethanol, and acetone were purchased from Xylong Company.

1 g of glucose and 0.5 g of citric acid were dissolved in 15 ml of distilled water. The obtained solution was then poured into a 25 ml Teflon. Subsequently, the hydrothermal process was carried out for 12 h at 160 °C. After the hydrothermal process was completed, the Teflon was cooled naturally to room temperature. Finally, the obtained solution was filtered through Whatman 3 µm paper to remove the large residue after the reaction. The final solution was the aqueous suspension of CDs. The solution had a pale yellowish color and was stored at 4 °C for further use.

The morphology of the CDs was characterized by high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM, JEM 2100, JEOL). The functional groups were determined using FT-IR spectroscopy (Affinity-1S spectrometer, Shimadzu, Kyoto, Japan), and the UV–Vis absorption spectrum was recorded using UV 2450-PC, Shimadzu – Japan. Photoluminescence spectroscopy (Fluorolog FL3, Jobin Yvon Spex) was used to characterize the optical properties of the prepared CDs. Raman spectrum of CD solutions was characterized by a BWTek Portable Raman System: i-Raman Pro model BWS475-785H.

Nguyen Minh Hoang: Writing – original draft, Formal analysis, Data curation. Nguyen Thi Bich Ngoc: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Data curation. Phan Thi Lan Huong: Formal analysis. Quang-Duy Dao: Methodology. Tran Nam Anh: Writing – original draft, Formal analysis, Data curation. Dang Thi Hai Linh: Data curation. VanThan Nguyen: Formal analysis. Le Tuan Tu: Writing – review & editing, Conceptualization. Ho Xuan Nang: Writing – review & editing, Conceptualization. Van-Duong Dao: Conceptualization, Investigation, Supervision, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We acknowledge the financial support from Vietnam National Foundation for Science and Technology Development (NAFOSTED) under grant number 103.02-2021.106.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.inoche.2023.111944.
